## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

message.content:
Would the following command only adjust the missing files or would it overwrite current ones?


bhyslop@DS223j:~$ rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --info=progress2 \
>   -e "ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -o IdentitiesOnly=yes" \
>   --rsync-path="/usr/bin/rsync" \
>   "/volume1/homes/bhyslop/bhyslop-vault/" \
>   "bhyslop@192.168.1.247:/volume1/homes/bhyslop/bhyslop-vault/"

content.text:
It **can overwrite** — it's not limited to missing files.

With `-a` (which implies `-t` preserving mtimes), rsync uses the **quick check**: compare size + mtime. For each file on the source:

- **Missing on destination** → transferred (the 3 files you want)
- **Exists, size+mtime match** → skipped (the 13 shared files, based on your listings — sizes match and dates look identical)
- **Exists, size or mtime differs** → **destination is overwritten**

No `--delete`, so files only on .247 would be preserved (none in your case).

In practice, for your specific state, rsync will almost certainly just transfer the 3 missing files and skip the rest — the listings show matching sizes and mtimes. But "only adjust missing files" is not what the command guarantees.

If you want a hard guarantee of *only* missing files, add `--ignore-existing`:

```
rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --ignore-existing --info=progress2 \
  -e "ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -o IdentitiesOnly=yes" \
  --rsync-path="/usr/bin/rsync" \
  "/volume1/homes/bhyslop/bhyslop-vault/" \
  "bhyslop@192.168.1.247:/volume1/homes/bhyslop/bhyslop-vault/"
```

Or do a dry run first to see exactly what it would do:

```
rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --dry-run --itemize-changes ...  # same args
```

The `--itemize-changes` (`-i`) output shows one line per file with a code — `>f+++++++++` means new file, `>f.st......` means size/time differ (overwrite). That's the safest way to confirm before committing.

content.text:
Yes, safe to re-issue.

**State on .247 is unchanged** — still 13 files, same sizes, same 3 missing:
- `20080312-my-old-120G-drive.float1-1.7z`
- `20230207-float1-misc.7z`
- `20230207-float1-misc.broken1.7z`

No partial/temp dotfiles visible (no `.<name>.XXXXXX` hidden files in the listing). The directory mtime advanced from 06:27 → 06:34, which just reflects rsync touching the dir; no orphaned artifact remains.

Since you didn't use `--partial`, rsync cleaned up its in-flight temp file on cancel. Re-running with `--ignore-existing` will skip the 13 present files and start the 3 missing ones from zero.

One optional upgrade worth considering before kicking off ~279 GB over the network: add `--partial --append-verify` (or at least `--partial`) so the next cancel/interruption doesn't cost you the bytes already transferred:

```
rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --ignore-existing --partial --info=progress2 \
  -e "ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -o IdentitiesOnly=yes" \
  --rsync-path="/usr/bin/rsync" \
  "/volume1/homes/bhyslop/bhyslop-vault/" \
  "bhyslop@192.168.1.247:/volume1/homes/bhyslop/bhyslop-vault/"
```

With `--partial`, an interruption leaves the partial file in place; the next run resumes it. Without it, you restart from zero each time.

content.text:
**Not from this Mac — only by IP.**

- **.246** calls itself `DS223j` (the Synology model number — default hostname)
- **.247** calls itself `bhyslop-nas2`

But from here:
- No reverse DNS: `192.168.1.246/.247 → NXDOMAIN`
- No forward DNS: bare hostnames don't resolve
- No mDNS: `DS223j.local` and `bhyslop-nas2.local` also fail — Bonjour advertisement may be disabled on the NAS, or your Mac's mDNSResponder isn't seeing them on this segment

If you want hostname access, cheapest options:
1. **`/etc/hosts` entries** on this Mac (and anywhere else that needs them) — one-line fix, zero infrastructure
2. **Router-level DNS** — most home routers (UniFi, pfSense, etc.) let you add static host entries that everyone on the LAN inherits
3. **Enable Bonjour on the NAS** — DSM → Control Panel → File Services → Advanced → Bonjour; then `.local` names should resolve

Option 1 is the fastest if this is a one-machine problem; option 2 is the right fix if multiple devices need it.

lastPrompt: are the nas boxes addressible by hostname?

message.content: Would bonjour be dynamic?

content.text:
**Yes — that's the whole point of Bonjour.**

mDNS is link-local and dynamic by design:

- Each device multicasts its own name on `224.0.0.251:5353` (IPv4) whenever it joins the network or its IP changes
- Resolvers don't cache long — names always map to the current IP
- No central authority, no config file to maintain, no stale entries
- Survives DHCP lease renewals, router reboots, NAS IP reassignment

The tradeoff vs `/etc/hosts`:

| | Bonjour (`.local`) | `/etc/hosts` | Router DNS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Survives IP change | Yes, automatic | No — stale until you edit | Depends on router's DHCP reservation |
| Works from anywhere on LAN | Yes | Only on machines you edit | Yes |
| Works from VPN / off-LAN | No (link-local only) | Yes if VPN'd in | Yes if VPN uses router DNS |
| Setup cost | Enable once on NAS | Edit every client | One-time router config |

For your case — two Synology NASes whose IPs *could* shift if the router ever reassigns — Bonjour is the "set and forget" answer. `/etc/hosts` is fine too, but pair it with a DHCP reservation on the router so the IP never actually changes, otherwise you'll chase stale entries.

content.text:
**Your Mac's gateway is Google Nest Wifi / Google Wifi** — two signatures line up:

- Subnet `192.168.86.0/24` — Google's factory default for Nest Wifi / Google Wifi
- Gateway MAC `3c:28:6d:90:80:51` — OUI `3c:28:6d` is registered to **Google LLC**

But here's the wrinkle relevant to your DNS goal:

```
traceroute 192.168.1.247
 1  192.168.86.1   (your Nest Wifi)
 2  192.168.1.247  (the NAS)
```

Your Mac is on `192.168.86.0/24`; the NASes are on `192.168.1.0/24`. The Nest Wifi is in **router mode behind an upstream router** — it NATs your Mac's traffic out to the 192.168.1.x LAN where the NASes live. That's a **double-NAT topology**.

For DNS / hostname purposes this matters:

- The **Nest Wifi at 192.168.86.1** only owns names on 192.168.86.x. Adding entries there won't help anyone on the NAS subnet find the NASes by name.
- The **upstream router (probably 192.168.1.1)** owns the NAS subnet. That's where LAN-wide static DNS entries for the NASes would go.
- Google's app for Nest Wifi famously **does not expose static DNS entries** anyway — so even if it were the right router, that option is off the table. Only `/etc/hosts` or Bonjour would work on the Google side.

Want me to check what's at 192.168.1.1 to identify the upstream router?

content.text:
**Upstream router identified: Arris BGW320-505** (AT&T Fiber residential gateway, XGS-PON-capable — the current-gen replacement for the BGW210).

Topology summary:
```
Internet ─ BGW320-505 @ 192.168.1.254 ── NAS .246 (DS223j)
                 │                    └── NAS .247 (bhyslop-nas2)
                 │
                 └── Google Nest Wifi @ 192.168.1.x (WAN) / 192.168.86.1 (LAN) ── Your Mac
```

**What this means for naming the NASes:**

The BGW320 admin UI at `http://192.168.1.254/` lets you:
- ✅ **Reserve DHCP leases** (Home Network → IP Allocation) — lock .246 and .247 permanently to those IPs
- ❌ **Add custom DNS / hostname entries** — AT&T's firmware doesn't expose an arbitrary A-record UI

So the BGW won't give you "type `bhyslop-nas2` in a browser and reach it" by itself. But it can guarantee the IPs never shift, which makes the other two approaches rock-solid:

**Recommended combo:**
1. **DHCP reservation on BGW320** for both NASes → IPs locked forever
2. **`/etc/hosts` entries on your Mac** (and any other client that cares) → hostname access

If you want LAN-wide hostname resolution without editing every client, the cleaner path is to **enable Bonjour on the Synology DSM** (Control Panel → File Services → Advanced → Bonjour) — but be aware it's link-local, so your Mac (on 192.168.86.0/24 behind the Nest Wifi) probably still can't see multicast from 192.168.1.0/24. Bonjour dies at the NAT boundary.

Given your double-NAT: `/etc/hosts` + DHCP reservation is the path of least resistance.

message.content: I didn't realize until you pointed it out that there was a google wifi box in the mix: it has a spare ethernet.  Should I plug the nas there for long term?

content.text:
**Not straightforwardly — it creates more problems than it solves.**

Google Nest Wifi in its default "router mode" keeps its ethernet LAN ports on `192.168.86.0/24`. So if you plug a NAS into that spare port:

**What you'd gain:**
- That NAS is on the same subnet as your Mac → Bonjour `.local` actually works
- Mac ↔ that NAS: no NAT traversal, cleaner

**What you'd lose:**
- **The two NASes can no longer talk easily.** One stays on 192.168.1.x (BGW side); the other moves to 192.168.86.x (Nest Wifi side). Your current rsync between them would break — the 192.168.86.x NAS is now behind a NAT from the 192.168.1.x NAS's perspective, invisible without port forwarding.
- **Anything else on the BGW side** (other wired devices, guest phones on that WiFi) loses direct access to the moved NAS.
- **Still double-NAT overall**, just shuffled.

**Better long-term fix: put the Nest Wifi in bridge mode.**

Google app → Network settings → Advanced networking → WAN → "Bridge mode". That turns the Nest Wifi into pure WiFi APs on the BGW's LAN. Everything ends up on `192.168.1.0/24`:
- Both NASes stay together, rsync works
- Your Mac ends up on 192.168.1.x → Bonjour crosses no boundary → `DS223j.local` and `bhyslop-nas2.local` resolve
- No more double-NAT for anything
- DHCP reservations on the BGW cover all devices uniformly

Tradeoff of bridge mode: you lose Google's "prioritize this device", family WiFi schedules, and some guest-network niceties. Mesh still works between Nest Wifi units. For most homes that's an acceptable loss; if you use those features heavily it matters.

**Recommendation ranking:**
1. **Bridge-mode the Nest Wifi** — cleanest, permanent, enables Bonjour LAN-wide
2. **Leave topology alone, use `/etc/hosts` + BGW DHCP reservations** — zero structural change, one file to edit per client
3. Plug NAS into Nest Wifi spare port — **don't** unless you commit to using only one NAS

content.text:
Here's the sequence. Bonjour has two halves: **advertise** on the NAS, and **listen** on the client. Each side needs to work.

## Step 1 — Enable Bonjour on each Synology

On **both** NASes (.246 and .247) in DSM 7:

1. Log in to DSM web UI (`http://192.168.1.246:5000/` and `http://192.168.1.247:5000/`)
2. **Control Panel** → **File Services** → **Advanced** tab
3. Under **Bonjour**, check **Enable Bonjour service discovery**
4. (Optional, if you ever do Time Machine) Check **Enable Bonjour Time Machine Broadcast via SMB**
5. Click **Apply**

Each NAS will now advertise itself on its LAN segment as `<hostname>.local` → `DS223j.local` and `bhyslop-nas2.local`.

## Step 2 — The topology gotcha (must fix first)

**Bonjour is link-local.** It rides on multicast to `224.0.0.251:5353` and does not cross NAT or router boundaries. Right now:

- NASes multicast on 192.168.1.0/24
- Your Mac listens on 192.168.86.0/24 (behind Nest Wifi's NAT)
- The multicast dies at the Nest Wifi WAN → your Mac never hears the announcements

So enabling Bonjour alone won't make `DS223j.local` resolve from your Mac until **one** of these is true:

- **(Recommended) Nest Wifi in bridge mode** — collapses both subnets into 192.168.1.0/24, Bonjour works everywhere. Google Home app → Wi-Fi → Settings (gear) → Advanced networking → WAN → Bridge mode. *Warning:* this triggers a factory-style reconfigure; plan ~10 min of downtime and have the BGW handle DHCP afterward.
- **Move the Mac wired onto the BGW** (bypass Nest Wifi for that machine) — also puts you on 192.168.1.x.
- **Use an mDNS reflector** — e.g., `avahi-daemon` with `enable-reflector=yes` on a Linux box bridging the two subnets. More complex; skip unless you have a reason.

## Step 3 — Verify from the Mac

Once you're on the same L2 segment, from this terminal:

```
dns-sd -B _smb._tcp        # should list both NASes within ~1 second
ping -c 2 DS223j.local
ping -c 2 bhyslop-nas2.local
```

If those resolve, you're done. SSH, SMB, DSM web UI all accept `.local` names from that point.

## Suggested order for you

1. Enable Bonjour on both NASes now (2 min, harmless even before step 2)
2. Wait for the current rsync to finish (don't trigger any network reconfig mid-transfer)
3. Bridge-mode the Nest Wifi
4. Run the verify commands

Want me to verify the rsync is still on track before you kick off step 1?

- [What is Synology Bonjour? (Pros and Cons You Should Know!)](https://techguidecentral.com/what-is-synology-bonjour-pros-and-cons-you-should-know/)

message.content: She does use macs.  Is it easy to set her up to be able to use time machine?

content.text:
Yes — Synology + macOS Time Machine over SMB is a well-supported path, ~15 minutes of setup. Split across two sides:

## On the NAS (one-time, per NAS you want to use)

1. **Create a dedicated Time Machine user**
   Control Panel → User & Group → Create. Strong password. Grant her read/write on only the Time Machine folder (step 2).

2. **Create a shared folder for her backups**
   Control Panel → Shared Folder → Create. Name it `TimeMachine_<her-name>`. Enable **Recycle Bin: off** (TM manages its own snapshots; recycle just doubles storage). Leave Btrfs / encryption as you prefer.

3. **Set a quota — critical**
   Edit the shared folder → **Advanced** → set a user quota for her account. Rule of thumb: **2–3× her Mac's internal disk size**. Example: 512 GB Mac → 1.5 TB quota. Without a quota, Time Machine will happily consume every byte on the NAS.

4. **Register the folder as a Time Machine target**
   Control Panel → File Services → Advanced → **Set Time Machine Folders** (the button right below the Bonjour checkboxes you just enabled). Pick the folder from step 2.

5. **Confirm SMB is enabled**
   Same page, **SMB** tab → "Enable SMB service" checked. Max protocol: SMB3. (Default.)

## On her Mac

If her Mac is on the same L2 segment as the NAS (i.e., Bonjour works for her):
- **System Settings → General → Time Machine → Add Backup Disk**
- The shared folder appears in the list → pick it → enter her NAS credentials
- Done. First backup runs; subsequent backups are incremental hourly.

If her Mac is *not* on the same segment (same Nest Wifi issue you have):
- Finder → **Go → Connect to Server** (⌘K) → `smb://192.168.1.246/TimeMachine_<name>` → enter creds, check "Remember this password in my Keychain"
- Then **System Settings → General → Time Machine → Add Backup Disk** → the mounted share now appears in the list

## Practical notes

- **First backup is slow** (hours, full disk dump). Wire her Mac to ethernet for this one — a 500 GB first backup over WiFi can take a day.
- **Encrypt the backup** when adding it (macOS prompts). Separate password from the NAS account; required if the NAS is ever lost/stolen.
- **One TM target per Mac** per folder is cleanest. If you want multiple Macs backing up to one NAS, create one folder per Mac and list all of them in Set Time Machine Folders.
- **If she travels**, Time Machine gracefully skips when the NAS is off-network and resumes when she's home.
- **DSM 7 has no "Time Machine vault" package anymore** — the File Services Advanced tab is the whole story now. Older guides mention a "Time Backup" app; ignore those.

Want me to walk through creating the user + folder on one of the NASes now, or wait?

message.content: how can I learn how big my sister's mac's hard drive is?

content.text:
Easiest — have her send a screenshot of one of these:

**Option A (quickest, one screen):**
- **Apple menu** (top-left corner) → **About This Mac**
- In recent macOS: click **More Info…** → scroll to **Storage**
- In older macOS (Monterey and earlier): **Storage** is a tab at the top

She'll see a bar showing total disk size and how much is used. Screenshot that.

**Option B (more detail):**
- **System Settings** → **General** → **Storage**

Both show the same two numbers you care about:
- **Total capacity** — sizes the Time Machine quota (multiply by 2–3)
- **Used** — predicts first-backup duration

**If you want exact bytes and she's comfortable with Terminal:**
```
df -h /
```
The `Size` column is total, `Used` is occupied. Paste you the one line.

**What sizes you'll typically see on a modern Mac:**
| Model era | Common sizes |
|---|---|
| MacBook Air (M-series) | 256 GB, 512 GB, 1 TB |
| MacBook Pro (M-series) | 512 GB, 1 TB, 2 TB, 4 TB |
| Mac mini / iMac | 256 GB – 2 TB |

For a 256 GB Mac → ~512 GB–768 GB quota. For a 512 GB Mac → ~1–1.5 TB. For a 1 TB Mac → ~2–3 TB.

message.content: I accidentally connected the nas to the wrong tailscale network.  Can you help me detach and reattach it?

content.text:
Yes — straightforward. Two paths, depending on your preference:

## Path A: CLI via SSH (faster if you're already in a terminal)

On the affected NAS:

```
sudo tailscale logout
sudo tailscale up
```

The second command prints an auth URL. Open it in a browser **signed into the correct Tailscale account**. That's what binds the node to the tailnet you want — the login identity determines the tailnet. Authorize the new node there.

Verify:
```
tailscale status
tailscale ip -4
```

## Path B: DSM GUI

1. DSM → **Package Center** → open **Tailscale**
2. Click **Sign out** (or equivalent — the exact wording varies by version)
3. Click **Sign in** → follow the browser flow → make sure you're signed into the correct Tailscale account in that browser session
4. Authorize the node

## Clean up the wrong tailnet

Either before or after the above, on **the old (wrong) tailnet admin console** → `login.tailscale.com` → **Machines** tab → find the NAS → delete it. Otherwise it lingers as a stale node in the wrong tailnet.

## Two things I should know before we dive in

1. **Which NAS?** `.246` (DS223j) or `.247` (bhyslop-nas2)?
2. **Do you have admin access to both tailnets** — the one to remove from, and the one to add to? You'll need to be signed into the correct account in your browser for the re-auth step.

Also: if you use **tagged devices** or **ACL-restricted auth keys** on the correct tailnet, you may want a pre-auth key ready. Say the word and I'll walk you through that instead of interactive login.

When you're back out:

Should show the NAS with an IP in the new tailnet's range and status `active`.

## Don't forget cleanup on the old tailnet

- `https://login.tailscale.com/admin/machines`

lastPrompt: One other problem: ds223j has some sort of stale caching: I deleted several files but it still looks full.  Thoughts?

content.text:
**Diagnosis complete: Synology Drive version history is eating your disk.**

- `/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/` = **3.5 TB** (the entire volume)
- Your visible user data (vault + ERROR + maybe) = only ~1.3 TB
- So **~2.2 TB is version history** — every edit/delete Drive has ever seen, retained because version retention is effectively unlimited

When you "delete" a file in a Synology Drive–synced folder, Drive moves the latest version to a tombstone but **keeps every prior version**. Storage doesn't drop until you rotate/purge those versions.

## Fix (DSM web UI)

1. DSM → **Main Menu** → **Synology Drive Admin Console**
2. Left sidebar → **Version Control** (label may be "Versioning" in some versions)
3. You'll see:
   - **Total size** consumed by versions
   - **Current policy** (default is "Keep all versions forever" — that's the trap)
4. Two actions, in order:
   - **Change the policy** to something finite: "Keep versions for X days" (30 is common) or "Keep N latest" (10 is plenty for disaster recovery)
   - **Click "Rotate Versions"** (or "Remove Old Versions" — wording varies by Drive version). This immediately purges everything now out of policy.
5. Wait a few minutes for the rotation to complete, then re-check `df -h /volume1`

## Worth asking before clicking

- **Is Drive even serving a purpose here?** Your primary use seems to be rsync + SMB. If Drive isn't actively synced to any client (desktop apps), you can disable versioning entirely, or unshare the Drive team folder and the space frees on its own.
- **Check what's synced**: Synology Drive Admin Console → **Team Folder** tab → see which folders are under Drive management. If `bhyslop-vault` is one of them and you don't need client sync, remove it from Drive.

Want me to peek at the Drive team folder list via SSH to see what's configured?

message.content: Time Machine is now an important use case.  Do I need to disable the 'Drive' feature to set that up?

content.text:
**No — they're independent services. You can run both.**

- **Synology Drive** = file-sync + version-history service (Dropbox-style)
- **Time Machine** = macOS backup over SMB into a shared folder

They don't interact. Enabling/using one doesn't affect the other.

## But two practical cautions

**1. Space.** TM needs ~2–3× the Mac's internal disk (for ~500 GB Mac → 1–1.5 TB). Right now you have 81 GB free. You'll need to reclaim space before TM has anywhere to live — and Drive's 2.2 TB of old versions is the fastest reclaim available.

**2. The Time Machine folder must NOT be inside a Drive-synced folder.** If TM's share is also under Drive management:
- Drive will version-track every change to TM's sparsebundle band files
- A single day's backup activity could create gigabytes of version history
- Space usage explodes catastrophically
- TM performance craters

Keep the TM folder outside any Drive team folder. When you create the shared folder `TimeMachine_<her-name>`, don't add it to Drive's team folder list.

## Suggested order

1. **Reclaim space from Drive first** (change retention → rotate versions) — free up room before creating anything new
2. **Decide whether you even want Drive running.** If nothing is actively syncing to it from a desktop client, consider turning the whole package off. The space reclaim is more aggressive if you uninstall Drive entirely (the `@synologydrive` subvolume can go away).
3. **Then create the TM folder** as a fresh shared folder, with quota, outside Drive

Want to check what's currently using Drive before deciding whether to reclaim vs uninstall?

toolUseResult.stdout: SynologyDrive

content.text:
Only `SynologyDrive` installed (no ShareSync, no other Drive variants) — clean uninstall is possible.

## Pre-flight check

Before uninstalling, sanity-check there are no active Drive clients you care about:

- DSM → **Main Menu** → **Synology Drive Admin Console** → **Connection** tab → shows any connected clients (Mac/Windows desktop apps, mobile devices)
- If the list is empty or only shows devices you don't care about, you're safe to proceed

**What survives the uninstall:** every current ("tip") file in team folders. Those live in the regular shared folders (your `homes/bhyslop/...`), not in `@synologydrive`. Only version history and Drive's internal metadata go away.

**What's lost:** all old versions, all Drive-tracked deletions (no "undo" after this).

## Uninstall steps

1. DSM → **Package Center**
2. Left sidebar → **Installed**
3. Click **Synology Drive Server**
4. Top right → **Stop** (wait for it to stop)
5. Same menu → **Uninstall** → confirm

Package Center runs the uninstall scripts that handle the `@synologydrive` subvolume cleanup cleanly. Don't use `synopkg uninstall` from CLI — it can leave the subvolume orphaned on some DSM versions.

## After uninstall

Wait ~1 min for subvolume cleanup to complete, then tell me and I'll verify:

```
df -h /volume1    # should drop from 3.5T used to ~1.3T used
ls /volume1/ | grep -i drive    # should show nothing
```

## One more thing

Once Drive is gone, there's no versioning safety net. If you want any "oops-undo" layer for the Time Machine folder (separate from TM's own retention), the DSM-native replacement is **Btrfs shared-folder snapshots** (Control Panel → Shared Folder → select folder → Snapshot). Lightweight, doesn't balloon storage like Drive did, and optional per-folder. Mention worth considering later — don't let it distract from the uninstall now.

Ready when you are. Say the word after uninstall completes and I'll verify the reclaim.

message.content: what is 'DSM'?  Where do I find it?

content.text:
**DSM = DiskStation Manager** — Synology's web-based admin interface. It's the "operating system" UI of the NAS, running as a web app on the NAS itself.

**You've already been in it.** The screenshot you sent earlier (the Bonjour checkboxes) was DSM. The browser tab said "DS223j - Synology NAS" and the URL was `192.168.1.246:5000`.

**To get back in:**

```
http://192.168.1.246:5000
```

(Or `https://192.168.1.246:5001` if HTTPS is enabled — same thing, encrypted.)

**Same admin credentials** you used for the Bonjour step.

## Landing inside DSM

Once logged in, you'll see a desktop-like environment with icons:

- **Control Panel** — where you did the Bonjour config
- **Package Center** — app store for Synology packages (this is where you go for the Drive uninstall)
- **File Station** — web file browser
- **Main Menu** — grid icon (top-left, four squares) → shows all installed apps including Synology Drive Admin Console

So for the uninstall:
1. Go to `http://192.168.1.246:5000`
2. Log in
3. Click the **Package Center** icon on the DSM desktop
4. Continue with the steps I gave in the previous message

**If it's still stuck at 3.5 TB after ~30 min**, there are a couple more escalations:
- Stale Synology shared-folder snapshots (would need `sudo btrfs subvolume list /volume1` to see)
- An orphaned subvolume Package Center missed (same command would show it)
- `synopkg uninstall --purge` residue

But likely we just need to wait. Ping me in 10–15 min and I'll re-check. Or say "check" whenever you're ready.

## Rsync progress — with a gotcha

- ✅ File 1 still complete (108.9 GB)

```
ssh bhyslop@192.168.1.247 'rm /volume1/homes/bhyslop/bhyslop-vault/20230207-float1-misc.7z'

```

Since file 2 no longer exists, `--ignore-existing` won't trip; rsync will transfer it from scratch (unfortunately — the 12 GB we already shipped is lost, but there's no clean way to resume it with this flag set).

**Alternative if you want to save those 12 GB:** drop `--ignore-existing` on the resume run. With just `--partial`, rsync resumes file 2 from 12 GB. But then rsync also checksums the 13 shared files to confirm they're identical — slower scan, ~10 min of seek-heavy I/O on both sides. Your call: 12 GB saved vs ~10 min extra scan.

- Before: 3.5T used, 82G free
- Now: **3.1T used, 436G free**

message.content: What setup is needed to enable Time Machine?

content.text:
Here's the concrete plan for .246 (DS223j) — since that's where you already enabled Bonjour TM Broadcast. Three questions to confirm before you click anything:

## Questions to answer first

1. **Which NAS?** DS223j (.246) is where we enabled the TM Bonjour flag. Sticking with it?
2. **Ann's Mac disk size?** (for sizing the quota — need to know before creating the folder)
3. **Tailscale vs LAN for her?** If she's often off-LAN, she'll need Tailscale anyway for TM to reach home. If she's always home, LAN-only is fine.

## Prerequisites (already done ✅)

- Bonjour TM Broadcast via SMB — checked earlier
- SMB service enabled — DSM default
- Drive package uninstalled, space coming back

## Steps to execute (DSM at `http://192.168.1.246:5000`)

### 1. Create a user account for Ann

**Control Panel → User & Group → User tab → Create**

- Name: `ann` (keep it simple; lowercase, no spaces)
- Email: her real email (useful for DSM notifications)
- Password: generate a strong one; save it somewhere before proceeding — you'll type it on her Mac
- **Join groups**: `users` (default). Do NOT add to `administrators`.
- **Assign shared folder permissions**: you'll configure after creating the folder in step 2; skip here or grant "no access" temporarily
- **Assign user quota**: skip — we'll enforce quota at the folder level, not user level

### 2. Create the Time Machine shared folder

**Control Panel → Shared Folder → Create → Create Shared Folder**

- Name: `TimeMachine_Ann` (or similar — avoid spaces)
- Location: Volume 1
- **Hide this shared folder in "My Network Places"**: off (needs to be visible over SMB)
- **Enable Recycle Bin**: **off** (TM manages its own history; recycle bin just doubles usage)
- **Encrypt this shared folder**: off (TM backups are encrypted client-side if you enable it on macOS; encrypting twice just costs CPU)
- **Enable data checksum for advanced data integrity**: on (Btrfs perk, cheap)

On the permissions step:
- `ann`: **Read/Write**
- Everyone else: **No access**

### 3. Set the folder quota

**Control Panel → Shared Folder → select `TimeMachine_Ann` → Edit → Advanced tab**

- **Shared folder quota**: enter a value = 2× to 3× Ann's Mac disk size
  - 256 GB Mac → 512 GB quota
  - 512 GB Mac → 1 TB quota
  - 1 TB Mac → 2 TB quota

The quota is enforced at the filesystem level — TM will fail gracefully when it hits the cap rather than filling your volume.

### 4. Register the folder as a Time Machine target

**Control Panel → File Services → Advanced → Set Time Machine Folders** (the button just below the Bonjour checkboxes)

- Check `TimeMachine_Ann` → OK → Apply

This tells SMB to advertise the folder with the special `_adisk` Bonjour record that triggers macOS TM auto-discovery.

### 5. On Ann's Mac

**System Settings → General → Time Machine → Add Backup Disk**

If LAN or Tailscale is working:
- The NAS will appear by hostname (`ds223j` or `DS223j.local`) → select the `TimeMachine_Ann` folder → enter `ann`'s credentials
- macOS will prompt to encrypt — **yes**, with a separate password (do not lose this one — required to restore)

If the NAS doesn't show up:
- Finder → **Go → Connect to Server** (⌘K) → `smb://192.168.1.246/TimeMachine_Ann` → enter credentials → check "Remember this password in my Keychain"
- Then re-try Add Backup Disk — the now-mounted share appears in the list

First backup: wire to ethernet if possible. A 500 GB first backup takes 2–8 hours on WiFi, 1–2 hours wired.

## Ready when you are

Tell me:
1. Ann's Mac disk size
2. Whether you want the folder named `TimeMachine_Ann` or something else
3. If DS223j is the target (vs. the other NAS)

message.content: She has multiple macs.  Do we provision a space for each?

content.text:
**Yes — one shared folder per Mac.** It's the clean pattern on Synology, for three concrete reasons:

1. **Per-Mac quotas.** The quota lives on the folder. Separate folders = separate caps, so one Mac can't squeeze out another.
2. **Per-Mac auditing.** `du -sh` on the folder = that Mac's backup footprint. Instant answer.
3. **Easy decommission.** Retire a Mac → delete its folder. Done, space returns.

(You *could* point multiple Macs at one folder — each creates its own sparsebundle inside — but then quota is all-or-nothing and cleanup gets messy.)

## Recommended structure

**One user account (`ann`), multiple folders** — simpler for her (one password), still isolated per-Mac:

```
TimeMachine_Ann-MBA       # her MacBook Air
TimeMachine_Ann-iMac      # if she has an iMac
TimeMachine_Ann-Mini      # etc.
```

Separate users per Mac is overkill for one person's devices — save that pattern for when the people differ, not the machines.

## The repeat-per-Mac steps

Steps 1 (user) is done once. Steps 2, 3, 4 repeat per Mac:

| Step | Once | Per Mac |
|---|---|---|
| Create user `ann` | ✅ | |
| Create shared folder `TimeMachine_Ann-<mac>` | | ✅ |
| Set folder quota (2–3× *that* Mac's disk) | | ✅ |
| Add folder to **Set Time Machine Folders** list | | ✅ (add to existing list) |
| On the Mac: Add Backup Disk → pick the matching folder | | ✅ |

Note step 4: the DSM **Set Time Machine Folders** dialog is multi-select. Add all her Macs' folders to the same list in one Apply.

## Sizing total reservation

Add up your total TM budget across all her Macs:

- 512 GB MBA × 2.5 → 1.3 TB
- 256 GB iMac × 2.5 → 640 GB
- etc.

Then make sure that sum + your own data + your own TM if you ever want one all fits under your ~3.5 TB volume.

## Before you start

Tell me the list of Macs and their sizes. I'll give you the exact quota numbers and folder names to type.

message.content: One of her computers is about to be decomissioned.  Should we size a time machine directory a little larger than her storage only?  Is a time machine image accessible without the mac?

content.text:
Two separate considerations — both worth thinking through.

## Sizing for a decommission-bound Mac

**Yes, you can size it tight.** The 2–3× rule exists because ongoing TM keeps history: hourly for 24h, daily for a month, weekly forever. For a one-shot "last backup before retirement," none of that matters — you just need room for the current contents.

**Rule of thumb for archive-only:**
```
quota = used_space × 1.2
```
The 20% overhead covers sparsebundle metadata (~5%), macOS invisible files, and some wiggle room. If she's at 180 GB used, a 220 GB quota is plenty.

Check "used" not "total" — Apple menu → About This Mac → Storage gives you the exact "Used" number.

**Caveat:** if she's going to use the Mac for another few weeks before retiring it and wants incremental-history behavior during that window, stay at 2×. Tight sizing assumes "one final backup, then pull the plug."

## Is a TM backup accessible without the original Mac?

Depends on what "accessible" means. Four scenarios:

| Scenario | Without original Mac? |
|---|---|
| **Restore to a new Mac** via Migration Assistant | ✅ Yes. Any Mac can point Migration Assistant at the sparsebundle and pull users/apps/files over. This is the primary "Mac dies, I buy new Mac" workflow. |
| **Browse individual files** on any Mac | ✅ Yes. Double-click the `.sparsebundle` on the NAS share → mounts as a disk → Finder shows all TM snapshots as folders → drag any file out. |
| **Read from a Linux or Windows box** | ⚠️ Technically possible (`sparsebundlefs` on Linux, plus `hfsprogs` for the inner filesystem) but painful. Not a path you'd want to rely on. |
| **Read without the encryption password** (if you enabled encryption) | ❌ No. Encrypted sparsebundles are sealed. Lose the password, lose the archive. |

## Practical recommendation for a retirement-bound Mac

If this is a true "archive and never touch again" scenario, I'd actually suggest doing **both**:

1. **TM backup** (what you're planning) — sized tight, encrypted, gives you Migration Assistant restore path
2. **Plain-file copy** of her home folder to a separate NAS folder — rsync or drag-to-Finder. Inspectable forever without any Apple tooling, survives any future Mac-ecosystem changes

The plain copy is cheap insurance. If you ever need to pull "that one PDF she had in 2026," you don't need a working Mac + the sparsebundle + the password — just Finder on any machine.

Useful rule: **backups you can't browse in 10 years aren't really backups.**

Want to set up the plain-file copy alongside the TM folder? It's just another shared folder with a different sync method (Finder or a one-shot rsync).

message.content: If we set time machine up on it, then synced, could you extract the plain file copy direct from the NAS?

content.text:
**Yes — from any Mac, not just the original one.** And I can help orchestrate it from this machine.

## How it works

The `.sparsebundle` that TM creates on the NAS is just a disk image. Any Mac can mount it:

```
# Mount the NAS share
open smb://192.168.1.246/TimeMachine_Ann-MBA

# Double-click the sparsebundle → it mounts as a disk in Finder
# Or CLI:
hdiutil attach "/Volumes/TimeMachine_Ann-MBA/Ann's MacBook Air.sparsebundle"
```

Once mounted, it looks like a normal disk. Inside you find:

```
/Volumes/Time Machine Backups/
  Backups.backupdb/
    Ann's MacBook Air/
      2026-04-22-140000/     ← each snapshot is a dated folder
      2026-04-22-150000/
      ...
      Latest/                 ← symlink to newest snapshot
        Macintosh HD/
          Users/
            ann/              ← her entire home folder, plain files
```

That "Latest" tree is the plain-file copy you want. You can rsync it right back to the NAS as a plain folder:

```
rsync -avH --progress \
  "/Volumes/Time Machine Backups/Backups.backupdb/Ann's MacBook Air/Latest/Macintosh HD/Users/ann/" \
  "/Volumes/TimeMachine_Ann-MBA-PlainCopy/"
```

## Caveats

- **If you encrypted the TM backup** (recommended earlier): you need the encryption password to mount. Without it, sealed.
- **APFS vs HFS+ inside**: modern Macs produce APFS-inside-sparsebundle; macOS mounts both transparently, no extra tools needed.
- **Hardlinks**: TM uses aggressive hardlinking to save space. The `-H` flag on rsync preserves them; without it, your plain copy will balloon in size. Keep `-H`.
- **Network-of-bands overhead**: the sparsebundle is thousands of small `band` files over SMB. Mounting and reading is slower than rsync-of-native-files would have been.

## But — pragmatic alternative

If the plain-file copy is the goal, **skip the TM round-trip**. Direct rsync from her Mac to the NAS gives you the same result, faster, no encryption password to manage, no mount-and-extract step:

```
# From her Mac, one-shot archive:
rsync -aHX --info=progress2 /Users/ann/ bhyslop@192.168.1.246:/volume1/TimeMachine_Ann-MBA-PlainCopy/
```

## The "belt-and-suspenders" combo

If you want both restore-ability AND plain-file browsability:

1. Set up TM normally (tight quota, ~1.2× used) — gives Migration Assistant restore path
2. Also run a one-shot rsync of her home folder into a separate NAS folder — gives plain inspectable archive
3. Neither depends on extracting from the other

The extract-from-TM path works, but it's a rescue technique — not something you want to do routinely. Direct rsync is the clean tool for the "plain-file archive" use case.

Want me to stage both folders on the NAS (TM target + plain-copy destination) once you confirm the sizes?

message.content: Tell me about Tailscale System Extensions.  Is that needed on my sister's mac that we are snapshotting for decomission?

content.text:
## What Tailscale System Extensions are

macOS has two ways to deliver Tailscale:

| Distribution | Mechanism | Approval |
|---|---|---|
| **Mac App Store** | Network Extension (app-bundled) | None — installs like any App Store app |
| **Direct download from tailscale.com** | System Extension | Requires admin approval in System Settings → Privacy & Security, sometimes a reboot |

System Extensions are Apple's replacement for the old kernel extensions — privileged code that lives outside the app sandbox and hooks into networking more directly. Tailscale's standalone build uses one to implement the VPN tunnel at a lower level than the App Store sandbox allows.

**Practical differences:**

- App Store version: install, click OK, done. Missing a few features (e.g., exit-node-as-this-machine, some advanced routing).
- Standalone: more capable, but first install pops a "System Extension Blocked" dialog → System Settings → Privacy & Security → "Allow". On Apple Silicon, enabling non-Apple System Extensions may require booting into Recovery the first time and enabling "Reduced Security" mode. One-time pain.

## For a decommission-bound Mac

**No — don't install Tailscale there just for the backup.** Two reasons:

1. **Physical proximity wins.** If the Mac can be brought to the NAS's LAN (same house/office), wired gigabit ethernet is faster and simpler than any Tailscale path. Plug in, mount SMB at `smb://192.168.1.246/TimeMachine_Ann-<name>` or directly rsync, run the backup, pull the plug.

2. **System Extension hassle is overhead you don't need.** For a one-shot operation, installing → approving → possibly rebooting-into-Recovery just to set up a Tailscale exit route is effort that buys you nothing versus ethernet.

## When Tailscale *would* make sense

- She lives far away and the Mac can't easily be brought to you
- You want her to run backups on a schedule from wherever she is (ongoing TM scenario)
- She'll keep using the Mac from multiple locations

In that case, **pick the App Store version** — it avoids the System Extension approval flow entirely and still handles Time Machine over SMB just fine. The advanced features only the standalone version has (subnet routes, exit nodes) aren't relevant for "back up my home folder to the NAS."

## Concrete recommendation for the decommission

1. Bring the Mac to the LAN
2. Plug in ethernet (or same WiFi as the NAS)
3. Test: `ping 192.168.1.246` — should work in one hop
4. Do the backup (TM or rsync)
5. Unplug, retire the Mac

No Tailscale install, no System Extension dialog, no reboots. 30 minutes of setup cut to 0.

message.content: Help me uninstall the one I downloaded from tailscale so I can switch to app store variant.

content.text:
Straightforward. Which Mac are we doing this on — this mac mini (where I have shell access) or another machine?

The procedure is the same; I can just run some of it for you if it's this one.

## Steps

### 1. Sign out (detaches the node cleanly from the tailnet)

- Click the **Tailscale menu bar icon** (top right, near the clock)
- Menu → **Sign out**

### 2. Use Tailscale's built-in uninstaller

Recent Tailscale versions ship with a clean uninstall that handles the System Extension properly:

- Tailscale menu bar icon → **"..." menu** (or gear icon, depending on version) → **"Uninstall Tailscale..."**
- Approve the admin prompt when macOS asks
- The System Extension is removed along with the app

### 3. Verify the System Extension is gone

```
systemextensionsctl list
```

Should show no `io.tailscale` entries. If one lingers marked `[activated waiting for user]` or similar, continue to step 4.

### 4. Clean up leftover System Extension (only if step 3 found something)

```
# Find it:
systemextensionsctl list | grep -i tailscale

# Remove it (requires admin; may prompt):
sudo systemextensionsctl uninstall <team-id> io.tailscale.ipn.macsys
```

On Apple Silicon, if macOS complains about Reduced Security, the cleanest reset is: reboot → Recovery Mode (hold power at startup) → Startup Security Utility → set "Reduced Security" → reboot back and retry. **Only do this if stuck.**

### 5. Remove the stale VPN profile

System Settings → **VPN** → if a "Tailscale" entry lingers → minus button (−) to remove.

### 6. Verify app is gone

```
ls /Applications/ | grep -i tailscale    # should be empty
which tailscale                            # should return nothing
```

### 7. Delete the old node from the Tailscale admin console

Before installing the App Store version, delete the current Mac's entry at:

`https://login.tailscale.com/admin/machines` → find your Mac → `...` → **Remove machine**

Otherwise the App Store install creates a new node like `<hostname>-1`, and you accumulate stale entries.

### 8. Install App Store version

Mac App Store → search "Tailscale" → install → sign in with the same account → authorize the new node.

Verify:

```
tailscale status
```

---

**Tell me which Mac** and I can run the verification/cleanup bits over SSH for you if it's this mac mini. For a different machine, you'll drive it locally.

message.content: Removal with systemext... failed with "At this time, this tool cannot be used if System Integrity Proteciton is enabled."

content.text:
That message is telling you you're on the wrong path — don't disable SIP. The `systemextensionsctl` CLI is a nuclear-option tool that only works with SIP off, which you shouldn't do.

**The right way is app-initiated uninstall.** Only the owning app can ask macOS to remove its own System Extension, and macOS trusts that request without SIP changes.

## What to do instead

### First, see what state it's actually in

```
systemextensionsctl list | grep -i tailscale
```

Paste me the output. The key column is the state: `[activated enabled]`, `[terminated waiting to be activated]`, `[activated waiting for user]`, etc. That tells us whether the extension is live or already halfway dead.

### Then the proper removal, depending on what you see

**Case A — Tailscale.app is still in `/Applications/`:**

Open Tailscale → menu bar icon → look for **"Uninstall Tailscale..."** (in recent versions under the "..." / gear menu, or under Preferences → a button). That runs the in-app uninstaller which has permission to remove the extension.

**Case B — You already deleted Tailscale.app (hence the CLI attempt):**

You need to **reinstall it temporarily** just to run its uninstaller:

1. Download the standalone DMG again: `https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/#macos` (or the tailscale.com macOS download link)
2. Drag to Applications — yes, back to where you removed it from
3. Launch it — you don't need to sign in
4. Use its built-in **"Uninstall Tailscale..."** option
5. The extension gets cleanly removed

It's counterintuitive ("reinstall to uninstall?"), but that's the design constraint Apple enforces: only the app that owns an extension can remove it.

### Why this is the only sane path

- Keeps SIP enabled (don't disable it for a one-shot cleanup)
- No Recovery Mode boots
- Works on both Intel and Apple Silicon the same way
- The extension goes away the same session, no reboot required

Run the `systemextensionsctl list` check first and tell me what state it shows — that determines whether you need Case A or Case B.
